meta.description: MVP test slice #4. Covers freedom of the press doctrine focusing on the defamation/actual malice line from NYT v. Sullivan (1964) through Milkovich (1990). Prior restraint cross-slice connections modeled via RELATED_TO and cross-slice edges. Reporter's privilege (Branzburg line) deferred to Phase 2 pending statute layer. Five schema issues surfaced (#19-23).

areas.label: Freedom of the Press › Doctrine
areas.description: The First Amendment's protection of the press as an institution and of press functions — newsgathering, publication, and dissemination of information on matters of public concern. Freedom of the press doctrine overlaps substantially with free speech doctrine but has distinct rules in three areas: (1) the actual malice standard governing defamation claims against press defendants; (2) the prior restraint doctrine protecting publication from advance suppression; and (3) the newsgathering privilege protecting the press's ability to gather information. The press does not have rights categorically greater than those of ordinary citizens, but press functions receive strong First Amendment protection.
areas.notes: valid_from reflects NYT v. Sullivan (1964), which established the constitutional framework for press freedom in defamation cases. Prior restraint doctrine (Near v. Minnesota, 1931) predates this but is modeled in the prior_restraint slice. The Press Clause and Speech Clause of the First Amendment are textually distinct but courts have rarely treated press freedom as categorically different from speech freedom — Branzburg v. Hayes (1972) declined to recognize a special reporter's privilege beyond what ordinary citizens have.

doctrines.label: Actual Malice Doctrine
doctrines.description: The First Amendment prohibits a public official or public figure from recovering damages for defamation unless the plaintiff proves the defendant made the statement with actual malice — that is, with knowledge that it was false or with reckless disregard of whether it was false or not. The actual malice standard is a constitutional floor that preempts state defamation law when the plaintiff is a public official or public figure. It applies to both compensatory and punitive damages. The standard is subjective — it asks what the defendant actually knew or believed, not what a reasonable person would have known.
elements: Plaintiff must be a public official, all-purpose public figure, or limited-purpose public figure
Statement must concern the plaintiff's public role or the matter that made them a public figure
Plaintiff must prove by clear and convincing evidence that defendant knew the statement was false, OR defendant acted with reckless disregard for the truth or falsity of the statement
Reckless disregard is subjective: defendant must have entertained serious doubts about the truth of the statement
Actual malice standard applies to both liability and to punitive and presumed damages
doctrines.notes: SCHEMA ISSUE A: The actual malice standard applies differently based on plaintiff category — public official, all-purpose public figure, limited-purpose public figure, private figure. This four-category taxonomy is central to the doctrine's application but the schema has no way to represent it structurally. It functions like a sub-test within the doctrine but doesn't fit the DoctrinalTest model cleanly because it's a classification framework, not a sequential multi-prong test. Candidate for promotion to a first-class node type or a structured sub-schema.

doctrines.label: Private Figure Defamation Doctrine
doctrines.description: When a private figure plaintiff sues a media defendant for defamation on a matter of public concern, the First Amendment requires at minimum that the plaintiff prove the defendant was at fault — negligence or higher — and limits recovery to actual damages unless the plaintiff proves actual malice. States may set the standard above negligence but may not impose strict liability. When the matter is not of public concern, the constitutional floor is lower and states have more latitude. This doctrine creates a federal constitutional minimum above which state defamation law operates.
elements: For matters of public concern: plaintiff must prove at minimum that defendant was at fault (negligence standard)
States may impose a higher standard (actual malice) but may not impose strict liability
Recovery limited to actual damages unless plaintiff proves actual malice
For matters not of public concern: states have greater latitude; constitutional floor is lower
Presumed and punitive damages require proof of actual malice regardless of plaintiff's status
doctrines.notes: SCHEMA ISSUE E: This doctrine creates a federal constitutional floor with state law operating above it. The schema is scoped to federal constitutional law, but this doctrine is meaningless without reference to the state law it constrains. Gertz explicitly declined to impose the actual malice standard on private figure plaintiffs and left the standard to the states above the constitutional minimum. The graph needs a way to represent a doctrine that sets a floor for state law without fully occupying the field. This is a Phase 2 boundary condition — it requires the statute/state law layer to model completely.

doctrines.label: Opinion-Fact Distinction Doctrine
doctrines.description: Statements of pure opinion on matters of public concern that cannot reasonably be interpreted as stating actual facts are protected by the First Amendment. The protection is not grounded in a categorical 'opinion privilege' — Milkovich (1990) explicitly rejected such a privilege — but in the falsity requirement: a statement of opinion that cannot be proven true or false cannot satisfy the falsity element of defamation. The First Amendment also protects rhetorical hyperbole and statements that no reasonable person would interpret as stating facts. Context is critical: the same statement may be actionable in a news article but protected as opinion in an editorial or letter to the editor.
elements: Pure opinion on matters of public concern that cannot be proven false is protected
No categorical 'opinion privilege' exists — Milkovich rejected such a privilege
Statements that cannot reasonably be interpreted as stating actual facts are protected
Rhetorical hyperbole and satire are protected when context makes clear they are not statements of fact
Context determines whether a reasonable reader would interpret a statement as fact or opinion
doctrines.notes: SCHEMA ISSUE C: The opinion-fact distinction is a multi-factor standard without clean numbered prongs. Milkovich identified relevant factors (whether statement is objectively verifiable, whether the context signals opinion, whether the statement is capable of being proven true or false) but did not enumerate a formal test. The Doctrine vs. DoctrinalTest boundary question: is this a doctrine with contextual factors, or a test without clean prong structure? Currently modeled as Doctrine. If the research tool needs to display the factors as a structured checklist, it should be promoted to DoctrinalTest with a contested classification note.

doctrines.label: Reporter's Privilege Doctrine
doctrines.description: The First Amendment does not protect journalists from being compelled to testify before grand juries about their sources or newsgathering activities. Branzburg v. Hayes (1972) rejected a constitutional reporter's privilege, with Powell's concurrence leaving open the possibility of a more limited privilege in civil cases or before legislative committees. Most reporter's privilege protection in the United States comes from state shield laws and the federal common law, not from constitutional doctrine. This doctrine is modeled as a stub pending Phase 2 statute layer development.
elements: No absolute First Amendment privilege to refuse grand jury testimony about sources
Powell concurrence suggests possible balancing test in civil cases or legislative subpoenas
Constitutional protection, if any, is limited and context-dependent
Primary protection comes from state shield laws and federal common law, not the Constitution

pending_establishment.note: Branzburg is the foundational case for reporter's privilege doctrine. Modeled as stub here pending full reporters_privilege sub-slice which requires statute layer (Phase 2) to model completely.

doctrines.notes: TRANSITIONAL: This doctrine is modeled as a stub. Full modeling requires the Phase 2 statute layer because most reporter's privilege protection is statutory (state shield laws, Privacy Protection Act). The constitutional dimension (Branzburg line) can be modeled in isolation but is misleading without the statutory context. Defer full modeling until Phase 2.

doctrinal_tests.label: Actual Malice Test
doctrinal_tests.description: The constitutional standard for determining whether a public official or public figure plaintiff may recover damages in a defamation action against a press or media defendant. Plaintiff must prove by clear and convincing evidence that the defendant published the statement with knowledge of its falsity or with reckless disregard for whether it was true or false. The standard is subjective and defendant-focused: it asks what the defendant actually believed, not what a reasonable publisher would have believed.

prongs.label: Public official or public figure plaintiff
prongs.description: The plaintiff must be a public official, all-purpose public figure, or limited-purpose public figure. Private figures are governed by the Gertz standard, not actual malice. The categorization of who qualifies as a public figure is itself a contested doctrinal question with its own factors.
prongs.burden_note: Defendant may raise plaintiff's status as a threshold issue. Court determines plaintiff's status as a matter of law.
prongs.contest_note: The boundary between limited-purpose public figures and private figures is heavily litigated and inconsistently applied across circuits. A person becomes a limited-purpose public figure by voluntarily injecting themselves into a particular public controversy — but what counts as voluntary, and which controversies qualify, varies. SCHEMA ISSUE A: The public figure taxonomy (public official, all-purpose public figure, limited-purpose public figure, private figure) is a classification framework that functions as a precondition to the test. It does not fit cleanly as a prong because it is itself a multi-factor inquiry with its own sub-criteria. The schema has no way to represent a structured pre-test classification as part of a DoctrinalTest.

prongs.label: Knowledge of falsity or reckless disregard
prongs.description: Plaintiff must prove by clear and convincing evidence that: (a) defendant knew the statement was false at the time of publication, OR (b) defendant acted with reckless disregard for whether the statement was true or false. Reckless disregard requires proof that the defendant subjectively entertained serious doubts about the truth of the statement. Negligence — even gross negligence — is constitutionally insufficient.
prongs.burden_note: Clear and convincing evidence standard — higher than preponderance but lower than beyond reasonable doubt. Plaintiff must prove defendant's subjective state of mind, which is typically established through circumstantial evidence of the defendant's newsgathering process.
prongs.contest_note: The subjective recklessness standard — defendant must have entertained serious doubts — was established in St. Amant v. Thompson (1968). The standard for what constitutes 'reckless disregard' in the context of hot-button political reporting, social media republication, and AI-generated content is an active area of development. Some courts have struggled with applying a subjective standard to institutional defendants (corporations, news organizations) where no individual decision-maker had the requisite state of mind.

prongs.label: Statement of fact, not opinion
prongs.description: The statement must be capable of being proven false — i.e., it must be a statement of fact, not pure opinion or rhetorical hyperbole. A statement that cannot reasonably be interpreted as stating actual facts cannot satisfy the falsity element and is protected regardless of the speaker's intent.
prongs.burden_note: Whether a statement is fact or opinion is a question of law for the court. Plaintiff bears the burden of establishing the statement is capable of being proven false.

doctrinal_tests.notes: The actual malice standard has been extended beyond classic press defamation to: IIED claims by public figures (Hustler v. Falwell, 1988), false light invasion of privacy, and some tortious interference claims. The extension pattern is modeled through MODIFIES edges from the relevant cases.

doctrinal_tests.label: Public Figure Classification Test
doctrinal_tests.description: The framework for determining whether a defamation plaintiff is a public official, all-purpose public figure, limited-purpose public figure, or private figure — which in turn determines which constitutional standard governs the claim. Established across NYT v. Sullivan (public officials), Curtis Publishing v. Butts (public figures), and Gertz v. Robert Welch (private figures and the public figure taxonomy).

prongs.label: Public official
prongs.description: Government employees who have or appear to have substantial responsibility for or control over the conduct of governmental affairs. The category is not limited to elected officials — it includes appointed officials in positions with significant public trust. The line between public officials and low-level government employees is fact-specific.
prongs.burden_note: Defendant must demonstrate plaintiff held a position of significant governmental responsibility. Courts look to the nature of the position, not just its title.

prongs.label: All-purpose public figure
prongs.description: Individuals who have achieved pervasive fame or notoriety in the community — celebrities, major public personalities. These individuals are public figures for all purposes and contexts. They must prove actual malice for any defamatory statement, regardless of whether it concerns their public role.
prongs.burden_note: Courts apply this category narrowly. True all-purpose public figures are rare — most cases involve limited-purpose public figures.

prongs.label: Limited-purpose public figure
prongs.description: Individuals who voluntarily inject themselves into a particular public controversy and thereby become public figures for the limited range of issues related to that controversy. They must prove actual malice only for statements relating to the controversy that made them public figures.
prongs.burden_note: Most heavily litigated category. Courts assess: (1) whether a public controversy existed, (2) whether plaintiff voluntarily thrust themselves into it, (3) whether the alleged defamation relates to plaintiff's role in that controversy.
prongs.contest_note: The voluntary injection requirement and the scope of the relevant controversy are inconsistently applied. Some circuits require active voluntary participation; others find that being named in a public controversy, even involuntarily, can make someone a limited-purpose public figure. What constitutes a 'public controversy' distinct from merely a matter of public interest is also unsettled.

prongs.label: Private figure
prongs.description: Anyone who does not qualify as a public official, all-purpose public figure, or limited-purpose public figure. Private figure plaintiffs need only prove fault (negligence or higher, set by state law) and are limited to actual damages unless they prove actual malice.
prongs.burden_note: For matters of public concern: plaintiff proves fault at whatever standard state law imposes (minimum: negligence). For matters not of public concern: state law has broader latitude.

doctrinal_tests.notes: SCHEMA ISSUE A (second instance): This test functions as a threshold classification that determines which other test applies. It is a meta-test — a test about which test governs — rather than a test applied to the merits. The schema has no way to represent a DoctrinalTest that is a precondition to another DoctrinalTest rather than an independent test applied to the underlying facts. The GOVERNED_BY edge from freedom_of_press to this test is accurate, but the conditional relationship between this test and actual_malice_test is not expressible in the current edge vocabulary.

cases.full_name: New York Times Co. v. Sullivan
cases.court_name: Supreme Court of the United States
cases.holding: The First Amendment prohibits a public official from recovering damages for a defamatory falsehood relating to his official conduct unless he proves the statement was made with actual malice — knowledge of falsity or reckless disregard of whether it was false or not. State defamation law that lacks this constitutional protection is invalid as applied to criticism of the official conduct of public officials.

opinions.author: Black
opinions.doctrinal_significance: Black argued for an absolute privilege for criticism of public officials — no actual malice standard required, just complete protection. His position was not adopted by the majority but represents the absolutist wing of First Amendment theory.

opinions.author: Goldberg
opinions.doctrinal_significance: Goldberg also argued for absolute privilege, agreeing with Black's conclusion but through a slightly different rationale.

cases.notes: One of the most important First Amendment decisions ever issued. Sullivan arose from civil rights era Alabama, where segregationist officials were using defamation law as a tool to suppress civil rights coverage. Brennan's opinion constitutionalized defamation law for the first time, holding that the First Amendment places limits on state defamation claims when public officials sue over their official conduct. The 'profound national commitment to the principle that debate on public issues should be uninhibited, robust, and wide-open' is one of the most-cited passages in First Amendment jurisprudence. SCHEMA ISSUE B: Sullivan constitutionalized what had been a purely state common law tort. The schema models doctrines that govern speech — but this doctrine constrains a cause of action rather than directly restricting government power over speech. This is a structurally different pattern from all prior slices.

cases.court_name: Supreme Court of the United States
cases.holding: The actual malice standard applies to public figures — not just public officials — who sue for defamation. A plurality held that the standard for public figures is whether the defendant exercised 'highly unreasonable conduct constituting an extreme departure from the standards of investigation and reporting ordinarily adhered to by responsible publishers.' Harlan's plurality standard was subsequently superseded by Gertz, which applied the actual malice standard to all-purpose public figures.

opinions.author: Warren
opinions.doctrinal_significance: Warren's concurrence argued for applying the full Sullivan actual malice standard to public figures. This position was ultimately adopted in Gertz, making Warren's concurrence in Butts the doctrinal source for the public figure extension of actual malice.

cases.notes: Decided alongside AP v. Walker (1967) as companion cases. Harlan's plurality standard — highly unreasonable conduct departing from responsible publisher standards — was the dominant holding but was effectively superseded by Gertz (1974), which applied actual malice to public figures directly. Status: limited because Harlan's plurality standard is no longer operative; Warren's concurrence position (full actual malice for public figures) became law in Gertz. SCHEMA ISSUE: plurality opinion where the concurrence's reasoning, not the plurality's, became majority law in a subsequent case. The opinions[] array and INTELLECTUALLY_PRECEDES edge handle this but the status field does not fully capture 'partially superseded by subsequent majority adoption of a concurrence.'

cases.court_name: Supreme Court of the United States
cases.holding: A private figure plaintiff suing a media defendant for defamation on a matter of public concern need not prove actual malice to recover, but the First Amendment prohibits strict liability and requires at minimum a showing of fault. States may define the appropriate standard of liability as long as they do not impose liability without fault. Recovery for presumed and punitive damages requires proof of actual malice. The Court also established the public figure taxonomy: public officials, all-purpose public figures, limited-purpose public figures, and private figures.

opinions.author: Brennan
opinions.doctrinal_significance: Brennan argued the actual malice standard should apply to all defamation cases involving media defendants and matters of public concern, regardless of plaintiff's status. His dissent represents the view that Sullivan should have been extended further than the majority allowed.

opinions.author: White
opinions.doctrinal_significance: White argued the Court went too far in constitutionalizing defamation law — states should have been left free to develop their own standards. His dissent represents the anti-constitutionalization wing.

cases.notes: Gertz is the structural companion to Sullivan. Sullivan handled public officials; Gertz handled everyone else and established the taxonomy that determines which standard applies. The 5-4 vote with four justices dissenting in different directions reflects how contested the constitutionalization of defamation law was. Powell's majority opinion is careful and limited — it explicitly declines to apply actual malice to private figures and leaves states significant room to operate above the constitutional floor.

cases.court_name: Supreme Court of the United States
cases.holding: Public figures and public officials may not recover for intentional infliction of emotional distress based on publication of a parody or satirical piece without proving actual malice. The First Amendment's protection of speech on public figures extends beyond defamation claims to IIED claims — the constitutional standard is the same. Outrageous parody directed at a public figure on a matter of public concern is protected speech.
cases.notes: Hustler published a parody of a Campari ad depicting Jerry Falwell Sr. describing his 'first time' as a drunken encounter with his mother in an outhouse. Falwell sued for defamation and IIED. The Court held that public figures cannot use IIED claims to circumvent the actual malice standard — the constitutional protection follows the plaintiff's status and the nature of the speech, not the cause of action. This extends the Sullivan framework beyond defamation. Connects to Snyder v. Phelps (2011) in the core_political_speech slice, which applied similar reasoning to a private plaintiff.

cases.court_name: Supreme Court of the United States
cases.holding: The First Amendment does not require a separate categorical privilege for statements of opinion. However, statements that cannot reasonably be interpreted as stating actual facts are protected by existing First Amendment doctrine — including the requirement that a defamatory statement must be provably false. Rhetorical hyperbole and statements that no reasonable person would interpret as factual assertions are constitutionally protected without the need for a separate opinion privilege.

opinions.author: Brennan
opinions.doctrinal_significance: Brennan argued the majority's rejection of a formal opinion privilege left insufficient protection for opinion speech and that the Court should have recognized a broader constitutional protection for statements that a reasonable person would understand as opinion.

cases.notes: Milkovich settled the question of whether there is a separate First Amendment 'opinion privilege' — there is not. But the holding is less restrictive than it sounds: the falsity requirement and the actual malice standard together provide substantial protection for opinion speech without the need for a separate privilege. The case arose from a sports column accusing a wrestling coach of lying under oath — the Court held the statement was sufficiently factual to be actionable if false and made with actual malice.

edges.notes: The Press Clause of the First Amendment ('or of the press') is the textual grounding. Courts have generally not treated the Press Clause as conferring rights categorically different from those of ordinary speakers, but it provides the constitutional foundation for the distinctive press freedom doctrines modeled here.

RELATED_TO criteria satisfied: (1) Shared constitutional grounding — both GROUNDED_IN amendment_1, specifically the Press Clause. (2) Overlapping case law — Near v. Minnesota (1931) and NYT v. United States (1971) appear in both doctrinal lineages; Near is the foundational prior restraint case and the foundational press freedom case. (3) Complementary not hierarchical — prior restraint addresses the mechanism of pre-publication suppression; freedom of the press addresses post-publication liability and press-specific constitutional protections. Different analytical mechanisms protecting the same press freedom interest.

RELATED_TO criteria satisfied: (1) Shared constitutional grounding — both GROUNDED_IN amendment_1. (2) Overlapping case law — Sullivan's public concern analysis imports reasoning from the core political speech tradition; Snyder v. Phelps (core_political_speech slice) applies public concern doctrine in a press-adjacent context. (3) Complementary not hierarchical — core political speech doctrine governs what speech is protected; freedom of the press doctrine governs how press defendants are treated in defamation and related torts. Overlapping but not hierarchical — press freedom is not a sub-category of core political speech.

SCHEMA ISSUE A: This GOVERNED_BY edge is accurate but incomplete. The public_figure_classification_test is a threshold classification that determines which other test applies (actual_malice_test vs. negligence). The schema has no way to represent a DoctrinalTest that is a precondition to another DoctrinalTest. The conditional relationship between this test and actual_malice_test requires a new edge type or edge attribute.

Sullivan applied First Amendment protections against a state defamation judgment via the 14th Amendment. This is the incorporation mechanism — 1st Amendment applied against state through 14th Amendment Due Process Clause.

Sullivan distinguished the prior restraint context from the post-publication liability context. Near established press freedom from prior restraint; Sullivan established press freedom from post-publication defamation liability. Brennan's Sullivan opinion cited Near as part of the historical foundation for robust press protection.

Expands: Butts extended the constitutional protection — and the actual malice standard — from public officials to public figures. The plurality applied a different standard (highly unreasonable conduct) but Warren's concurrence, which was adopted in Gertz, applied full actual malice. The expansion is ultimately confirmed by Gertz.

Warren's Butts concurrence argued for applying full actual malice to public figures. Gertz's majority adopted this position, superseding Harlan's plurality standard from Butts. This is a clean INTELLECTUALLY_PRECEDES instance: a concurrence in an earlier case became the majority rule in a later case.

Clarifies: Gertz established that actual malice applies to public figures (confirming Warren's Butts concurrence, superseding Harlan's plurality standard) and established the public figure taxonomy that determines who must prove actual malice. It also clarified that actual malice governs presumed and punitive damages even for private figure plaintiffs.

Extends: Hustler extended the actual malice standard to IIED claims by public figures — a different cause of action (tort, not defamation) in a new legal context. The doctrine's prongs are unchanged; its applicability is expanded to a new category of claims. This is a clean use of the MODIFIES(extends) direction value added in v0.4.

Snyder v. Phelps extended Hustler's reasoning to private plaintiff IIED claims in a public concern context, distinguishing Hustler (public figure plaintiff) while reaching a similar result through the public concern doctrine. The doctrinal connection between these two cases is important for understanding the full scope of IIED protection for press-adjacent speech.

Clarifies: Milkovich added the opinion/fact distinction as prong 3 of the actual malice test by establishing that a statement must be capable of being proven false to be actionable. This clarifies the scope of the test without narrowing or expanding it.
Stub governance edge. reporters_privilege_doctrine is modeled as a stub pending Phase 2 statute layer. pending_establishment on the doctrine node documents the Branzburg dependency.
Reporter's privilege claims, to the extent they exist constitutionally, derive from the Press Clause of the First Amendment.

schema_issues_found.description: Meta-test problem: a DoctrinalTest that is a threshold precondition to another DoctrinalTest. The public_figure_classification_test is not applied to the merits of a defamation claim — it is applied first to determine which other test governs the claim. The schema has no way to represent a conditional relationship between DoctrinalTests: 'if public_figure_classification_test returns public_official or public_figure, then actual_malice_test applies; if it returns private_figure, then press_defamation_private_figure_doctrine applies.' This is a new pattern. Proposed fix: add a PRECONDITION_TO edge type between DoctrinalTest nodes, or add a conditional_test field to DoctrinalTest that specifies what governs when the test returns each classification. This is related to but distinct from the GOVERNED_BY condition attribute (which handles instrument-type conditionality, not test-sequencing conditionality).

Constitutional constraint on common law cause of action — new structural pattern. All prior slices modeled government restrictions on speech (statutes, court orders, administrative licensing). Sullivan constitutionalized a state common law cause of action (defamation) by imposing a constitutional standard on what private plaintiffs must prove. This is structurally different: the First Amendment is constraining a private lawsuit, not a government restriction. The schema's GOVERNS_BY and ESTABLISHES edges model doctrine that governs areas of law — but they don't capture the distinction between doctrine that restricts government action and doctrine that constrains private litigation. A research tool query for 'what governs this area' returns both types without distinguishing them, which is misleading for practice. Proposed fix: add a constraint_type field to Doctrine nodes: government_restriction | litigation_constraint | structural_rule.

Federal constitutional floor with state law superstructure. The private figure defamation doctrine sets a constitutional minimum (no strict liability, fault required) but explicitly leaves states free to set higher standards. The schema models federal constitutional law but this doctrine is incomplete without reference to the state law it constrains and the state variation it permits. The doctrine node correctly describes the federal floor but the graph has no way to represent the relationship between the federal floor and the state law superstructure. This is a Phase 2 boundary condition. Proposed fix: add a federalism_structure attribute to Doctrine nodes: federal_floor (federal minimum, state law operates above), federal_ceiling (federal maximum, state law operates below), federal_preemption (federal law displaces state law), federal_exclusive (state has no role). This is a relatively common constitutional law pattern and will recur in equal protection, due process, and other 14th Amendment doctrine.

Partially superseded plurality opinion — status enum inadequate. Butts is in an unusual status: Harlan's plurality holding (highly unreasonable conduct standard) was superseded by Gertz, but the case is not overruled because Warren's concurrence position (full actual malice for public figures) was adopted by Gertz. The case is simultaneously: (1) limited as to Harlan's plurality standard, and (2) the source of the rule adopted by Gertz via Warren's concurrence. The status: limited captures the first but not the second. There is no status value for 'partially superseded with surviving doctrinal contribution via concurrence.' Proposed fix: add a doctrinal_survival note field to Case nodes, and consider whether status needs a partially_superseded value distinct from limited.

Multi-factor standard without clean prongs — Doctrine vs. DoctrinalTest boundary. The opinion-fact distinction involves a contextual multi-factor analysis (is the statement objectively verifiable? does context signal opinion? is the statement capable of being proven true or false?) that courts apply as a totality-of-circumstances test rather than a sequential prong-by-prong analysis. It fits neither the Doctrine model (too structured) nor the DoctrinalTest model (lacks clean numbered prongs and sequential burden structure). Currently modeled as Doctrine. The promotion rule criteria suggest it might need its own treatment: it recurs across multiple areas (defamation, IIED, false light), has its own lineage, and is independently queried. Proposed fix: add a test_form field to DoctrinalTest: sequential_prongs (standard multi-prong test like Central Hudson or Brandenburg) | classification_framework (like public figure taxonomy) | totality_of_circumstances (like opinion-fact analysis) | balancing (like Mathews). This would allow DoctrinalTest to represent a wider range of structured legal analysis.
